explanation: An English sentence needs a subject. When you answer about your day, start with the dummy subject 'it': 'It was not tiring.' Don't drop the subject the way you can in Chinese.
correct_examples: It wasn't tiring at all.
It was a relaxing day.
context: Talking about whether the workday was tiring

my_sentence: I just need to take on English and technological classes.
correction: I just need to take language and technology classes.
explanation: Use 'take classes' (not 'take on' classes) for attending a course. Use the noun 'technology' as a modifier ('technology classes'), not the adjective 'technological'. And the umbrella term that pairs with 'technology' here is 'language classes', not 'English classes'.
correct_examples: I'm taking language and technology classes this year.
She takes an online coding class every weekend.
context: Which classes to take to improve skills

my_sentence: All AI models use Transformer as their basic.
correction: All AI models use the transformer as their base.
explanation: When you refer to a specific known technology in general, use the article: 'the transformer'. Also, 'basic' is an adjective; the noun you want is 'base' (or 'basis'): 'as their base'.
correct_examples: Most modern AI models use the transformer as their base.
This framework is the basis of the whole system.

my_sentence: The AI internalizes the underlying rules behind all the detail.
correction: The AI internalizes the underlying rules behind all the details.
explanation: 'Detail' here is countable and refers to many pieces of information, so use the plural 'details'.
correct_examples: He pays attention to all the details.
The rules behind all these details are simple.

my_sentence: Different languages can use in different projects.
correction: Different languages can be used in different projects.
explanation: The languages don't do the using; people use them. So you need the passive voice: 'can be used in'. 'Can use' would mean the languages themselves are using something.
correct_examples: Different tools can be used for different jobs.
This method can be used in many situations.
context: Choosing a programming language by scenario

my_sentence: You just need to learn one or two language.
correction: You just need to learn one or two languages.
explanation: Any number above one takes a plural noun: 'one or two languages'. The 'or two' makes it plural.
correct_examples: I only speak one or two languages.
Pick one or two topics to focus on.
context: How many programming languages to learn

my_sentence: He or she will feel angry to you.
correction: He or she will feel angry at you.
explanation: You are 'angry at' (or 'angry with') a person, not 'angry to' them. The fixed preposition for the target of anger is 'at' / 'with'.
correct_examples: My boss was angry at me for being late.
Don't be angry with her; it wasn't her fault.

my_sentence: Compare to number one or two, I think keeping your opinion to yourself is better.
correction: Compared to options one and two, I think keeping your opinion to yourself is better.
explanation: Use the past participle 'compared to' (not the base verb 'compare to') to introduce this kind of comparison; it works like a reduced clause meaning 'when (it is) compared to'. (assistant note: a recurring error for you.)
correct_examples: Compared to last year, sales are much higher.
Compared to the other options, this one is cheaper.
context: Comparing the three personality types in the worksheet

my_sentence: If some of your coworkers ask you some questions. And you keep your opinion to yourself, you will lose your opportunity.
correction: If some of your coworkers ask you about what you think, and you keep your opinion to yourself, you will lose your opportunity to become a manager.
explanation: Don't cut one complex sentence into fragments. Keep the 'if' clause, a comma, and the main clause together as a single flowing sentence instead of stopping after the 'if' part.
correct_examples: If your coworkers ask for your view, and you stay silent, you miss a chance to shine.
If it rains tomorrow, and the road floods, we'll cancel the trip.
context: Disadvantages of keeping opinions to yourself

my_sentence: It's easy for me to find a new job.
correction: It's easier for me to find a new job.
explanation: You were comparing this path with becoming a manager, so use the comparative 'easier', not the plain 'easy'.
correct_examples: It's easier for me to switch jobs as an engineer.
Learning is easier with a good teacher.
context: Why he prefers improving skills over becoming a manager

my_sentence: The team may miss a good idea and make a wrong decision.
correction: The team may miss a good idea and make some wrong decisions.
explanation: You were talking about decisions in general, not one specific decision, so use the plural 'decisions' (and 'some' instead of 'a').
correct_examples: Without feedback, the team may make some wrong decisions.
They made a few bad decisions early on.

my_sentence: They will lose some opportunities to get promotion.
correction: They will lose some opportunities to get promoted.
explanation: Use the verb form 'get promoted' (passive of 'promote') after 'to', not the noun 'get promotion'. If you want the noun, say 'get a promotion'.
correct_examples: She worked hard to get promoted.
He finally got a promotion this year.
context: Consequence of staying silent at work

my_sentence: They expect you finish it by yourself.
correction: They expect you to finish it by yourself.
explanation: The pattern is 'expect somebody to do something'. You need 'to' before the verb: 'expect you to finish'.
correct_examples: My manager expects me to finish the report today.
We expect the team to deliver on time.
context: What a boss expects when assigning a task
